case.title: Batch Synthesis of 3,5-Disubstituted Isoxazole in TBAB/EG (1:5) DES
case.protocol: Phenylacetylene (1a, 0.5 mmol, 1.0 equiv) and ethyl nitroacetate (2, 1.0 mmol, 2.0 equiv) were added directly to TBAB/EG (1:5) DES (1 mL) in an oven-dried 30 mL vial equipped with a magnetic stirring bar; no additional solvent, catalyst, base, or additive was used. The reaction mixture was stirred at 120 °C in an oil bath for 15 min, at which point full conversion (99%) to ethyl 5-phenylisoxazole-3-carboxylate (3a) was achieved via 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition of the in-situ-generated nitrile oxide intermediate with phenylacetylene. After the reaction, the mixture was quenched with water, extracted with dichloromethane, dried over MgSO₄, and the solvent removed under reduced pressure; the isolated NMR yield was 83% (quantified against 1,3,5-trimethoxybenzene as internal standard).
case.notes: Model-matrix benchmark for upstream/council combinations on the DES isoxazole protocol.

batch_record.reaction_description: 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition of in-situ-generated nitrile oxide intermediate with phenylacetylene to form ethyl 5-phenylisoxazole-3-carboxylate.
batch_record.raw_text: Phenylacetylene (1a, 0.5 mmol, 1.0 equiv) and ethyl nitroacetate (2, 1.0 mmol, 2.0 equiv) were added directly to TBAB/EG (1:5) DES (1 mL) in an oven-dried 30 mL vial equipped with a magnetic stirring bar; no additional solvent, catalyst, base, or additive was used. The reaction mixture was stirred at 120 °C in an oil bath for 15 min, at which point full conversion (99%) to ethyl 5-phenylisoxazole-3-carboxylate (3a) was achieved via 1,3-dipolar cycloaddition of the in-situ-generated nitrile oxide intermediate with phenylacetylene. After the reaction, the mixture was quenched with water, extracted with dichloromethane, dried over MgSO₄, and the solvent removed under reduced pressure; the isolated NMR yield was 83% (quantified against 1,3,5-trimethoxybenzene as internal standard).

chemistry_plan.reaction_name: 1,3-Dipolar Cycloaddition of Nitrile Oxide with Phenylacetylene

feed_streams.reasoning: Substrate and precursor feed for nitrile oxide generation

stages.post_stage_reasoning: To separate the organic product from the reaction mixture

mechanism_steps.description: Formation of nitrile oxide from ethyl nitroacetate

chemistry_plan.mixing_order_reasoning: Simultaneous addition to ensure in situ generation of nitrile oxide
chemistry_plan.confidence_notes: Confident in reaction mechanism and flow advantages; temperature sensitivity noted.

analogies.summary: A photochemical cyclization reaction proceeding through a nitrene intermediate mechanism was conducted using 465 nm blue LEDs in acetonitrile solvent at 25°C and 0.1 M concentration. The continuous-flow photoreactor operated with a 10-minute residence time and 0.1 mL/min flow rate, achieving a 65% yield without requiring a photocatalyst. In comparison to the batch process, which required 10 hours (600 minutes) and only achieved 55% yield, the flow setup delivered a remarkable 60-fold reduction in reaction time while improving yield by 18.2%. The flow advantage stems from superior light intensity control and more efficient photon delivery in the continuous reactor design. This represents a significant process intensification with both time and yield benefits for the nitrene-mediated cyclization.

chemistry.bond_formed: C-N and C-O bonds forming oxazinone ring
chemistry.scope_summary: Various aryl azides with electron-donating and electron-withdrawing substituents, α-ketoacids with ortho-, meta-, and para-substituted aromatic rings, one aliphatic example (pyruvic acid), trifluoromethyl and thiophenyl variants. Generally low to moderate yields (23-65% in flow system).

optimization_table.key_findings: 75% light intensity in flow device gave 65% yield vs 55% in batch

key_observations_supporting_mechanism: UV-vis spectroscopy shows absorption profiles for reactants 1a and 2a
Side product analysis reveals compounds 4 (4%), 5 (1%), 6 (3%), and 7 (2%)
Control experiments show that both azide and acid are required for product formation
Detection of compound 10 by GC-MS in reaction with thiol additive

translation_logic.batch_limitation: Longer reaction times (600 min vs 10 min) and lower yields (55% vs 65%)
translation_logic.flow_advantage: 60-fold reduction in reaction time (from 10 h to 10 min) with 18% yield improvement and better light intensity control
translation_logic.what_changed: Reaction time dramatically reduced from 600 min to 10 min, yield increased from 55% to 65%, light intensity optimized to 75%
translation_logic.what_stayed_same: Temperature (25°C), solvent (CH3CN), concentration (0.1 M), scale (0.1 mmol), light source (blue LEDs, 50W)
translation_logic.safety_improvement: Continuous flow provides better control of photochemical reactions and reduced exposure to UV light

figure_descriptions: Mechanistic scheme showing nitrene intermediate pathway

conflicts: Pass 4 figure shows wavelength as 456 nm while pass 3 states 465 nm - using 465 nm from optimization data
full_record.notes: Well-documented photochemical flow chemistry study with clear batch vs flow comparison. Minor wavelength discrepancy between different sections. Throughput calculated from flow rate and concentration. Scale-up demonstrated at gram scale with 10h residence time.

analogies.summary: This photocycloaddition reaction utilizes triplet sensitization with 2,2'-dimethoxythioxanthone (2,2'-MeOTX) as the photocatalyst at 1 mol% loading under 455 nm COB LED irradiation. The flow process operates in acetonitrile at 200°C with a 5-minute residence time and 6.0 mL/min flow rate using a FlowSyn steel coil reactor (30 mL volume) equipped with a 34 bar back-pressure regulator. Flow chemistry enabled daisy-chaining of the initial photochemical step with high-temperature thermal cascade reactions, dramatically improving yield from 16% in batch (2 hours) to 88% in flow with a 24-fold intensification factor. The high-temperature operation required for the thermal cascade was much safer to implement in the continuous flow reactor compared to batch processing.

chemistry.reaction_name: [2 + 2] photocycloaddition followed by thermal electrocyclic cascade
chemistry.bond_formed: C-C bonds in cyclobutane ring and subsequent cascade products
chemistry.scope_summary: Five aryl enamide derivatives tested (phenyl, 3-F, 3-Br, 3-OMe, 4-CF3) with yields of 63-68% for most substrates. The 4-CF3 derivative was resistant to the thermal fragmentation step.

optimization_table.key_findings: MeCN solvent with 0.5 M concentration at 200°C with 5 min residence time gave optimal results

figures.key_information: photochemical reactor setup with dual light sources positioned above a round bottom flask on a heating plate

The ease at which this could be purified by filtration represents one of the main benefits of acetonitrile over xylene, which would not be possible without the use of continuous flow technology. This result represents a 24 h productivity of 490 g. translation_logic.batch_limitation: Low yield (16%) and long reaction time (120 min) in batch mode
translation_logic.flow_advantage: Daisy-chaining photochemistry with high-temperature thermal cascade reactions enabled by flow technology, achieving high productivity
translation_logic.what_changed: Temperature increased to 200°C, residence time reduced to 5 min, back pressure of 34 bar applied
translation_logic.what_stayed_same: Solvent (MeCN), concentration (0.5 M), light source power (36 W)
translation_logic.safety_improvement: High temperature thermal cascade reactions safer in flow than batch

Flow schematic for daisy-chained photo/thermal process

full_record.notes: High confidence extraction with clear quantitative data from optimization tables. Flow process shows dramatic improvement in yield (16% to 88%) and time reduction (120 min to 5 min). Productivity of 20 g/h mentioned in key claim.

analogies.record_id: Ishitani 등 - 2023 - Liquid-phase Catalytic Continuous-Flow Aromatic Nitration Reactions with Conc. HNO3 on Modified Mixe.pdf

metadata.mechanism_type: Solid acid catalyzed liquid-phase continuous-flow reaction

analogies.summary: This study describes a solid acid catalyzed liquid-phase aromatic nitration reaction using concentrated nitric acid in a continuous flow system. The reaction was conducted at 120°C in dichloroethane (DCE) solvent at 1.0 M concentration using a packed-bed reactor with a volume of 7.85 mL and a flow rate of 0.91 mL/min. The flow process achieved 90% yield compared to 92% yield in the corresponding batch reaction conducted under similar conditions (120°C, 1 hour reaction time). The continuous flow approach enabled safe handling of the highly exothermic and potentially hazardous nitration reaction with concentrated nitric acid through better heat management and containment in the packed-bed reactor format. A back-pressure regulator was required to maintain proper reaction conditions and prevent solvent vaporization at the elevated temperature.

chemistry.mechanism_type: Solid acid catalyzed liquid-phase continuous-flow reaction
chemistry.bond_formed: Nitro group attachment to aromatic ring
chemistry.scope_summary: The catalyst was effective for a wide variety of aromatic compounds, achieving good to high yields for substrates such as benzene, alkyl benzenes, chloro- or iodobenzene, naphthalene, acenaphthene, and 1,3-benzodioxole.

optimization_table.key_findings: Using Mo-modified Ti-Zr mixed-metal oxide catalyst achieved high yields at WHSV of 4.0 h⁻¹.

figures.flow_path: Solution A and HNO3 are pumped into the Packed-bed Reactor Module, which includes Reactor A and Reactor B.
figures.key_information: Catalyst and Celite are packed in Reactors A and B with specified amounts.

steps.name: Kinetics & Residence Time
steps.summary: τ = 0.4 min, range [0.2–2.2] min, via analogy (IF = 42×)
warnings: Very short τ (0.36 min) — verify experimentally
Methods disagree: τ_analogy = 0.4 min vs τ_class = 1.5 min (4.2× difference). Experimental verification strongly recommended.

steps.name: Reactor Sizing
steps.summary: V_R = 0.18 mL, d = 1.00 mm, L = 0.23 m, Pe = 4 (⚠ axial dispersion)
warnings: Pe = 4 < 100 — significant axial dispersion. Reactor behaves closer to CSTR than PFR. Reduce d (smaller d → higher Pe) or accept lower conversion efficiency.

steps.name: Mass Transfer
steps.summary: t_mix = 101.3 s, Da = 82.691 — Mass-transfer limited — mixing is the bottleneck

values.interpretation: Mass-transfer limited — mixing is the bottleneck

warnings: Da = 82.7 ≫ 1: severely mass-transfer limited. Need micromixer, smaller d, or pre-mixed streams.
assumptions: D = 1e-09 m²/s (typical for small organic molecules in liquid)

steps.name: Heat Transfer
steps.summary: Q_gen = 0.0886 W, Q_rem = 2.1432 W, Da_th = 0.0413 — Thermally safe

steps.name: Back-Pressure Regulator
steps.summary: BPR REQUIRED: 5.0 bar (Gas-liquid system detected — BPR mandatory to maintain gas solubility (Henry's law: C_gas = H·P) and prevent uncontrolled degassing.)
equations: C_{\mathrm{gas}} = H \cdot P_{\mathrm{partial}}\quad\text{(higher BPR} \Rightarrow \text{more dissolved gas)}

steps.name: Process Metrics
steps.summary: STY = 13.8333 mol/(L·h), P = 2.490 mmol/h, ṅ_lim = 0.0333 mmol/min, startup waste = 0.5 mL, IF = 42×

proposal.chemistry_notes: Key mechanism-driven decisions include maintaining precise temperature control for the nitrile oxide intermediate. The flow setup enhances heat transfer and reaction time control. The use of a coil reactor supports the thermal requirements of the reaction.
